name: Toyotama-hime
shortDescription: Dragon princess who married the hunter Hoori and is an ancestor of the Japanese Imperial line.
description: Toyotama-hime (also known as Princess Luxuriant Jewel) is a significant figure in Japanese mythology, appearing primarily in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. She is the daughter of the dragon king Ryūjin, who rules the sea. Toyotama-hime is known for her marriage to the hunter Hoori (also known as Yamasachi-hiko), a grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu. According to the myth, Hoori lost his brother's fishhook and traveled to the sea bottom in search of it, where he met and married Toyotama-hime. They lived happily for three years in the dragon palace. Eventually, Hoori found the fishhook and decided to return to the land. Toyotama-hime accompanied him, and she became pregnant. When she was about to give birth, she asked Hoori not to look at her during the delivery. However, Hoori could not resist his curiosity and peeked, witnessing her transforming into a dragon or a shark (depending on the version of the myth). Ashamed, Toyotama-hime returned to the sea, leaving behind her child, Ugayafukiaezu, who became the father of Emperor Jimmu, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Toyotama-hime represents the connection between the imperial family and the divine realm.
subtitle: Dragon Princess, Ancestor of Emperors
role: Ancestral deity, fertility goddess, connection to the sea

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Toyotama-hime's story is primarily detailed in the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle. Her father is Ryūjin, the dragon king of the sea. The main myth involving Toyotama-hime revolves around her marriage to Hoori. Hoori had lost his brother's fishhook and ventured underwater to find it. He met Toyotama-hime, and they fell in love. They married and lived in Ryūjin's palace for three years. After Hoori found the fishhook, they returned to land where Toyotama-hime conceived. She pleaded with Hoori not to observe her childbirth, but he broke his promise. Upon peeking, he saw her transform into a dragon or shark. This revelation caused her immense shame, and she returned to the sea, entrusting her child to her younger sister, Tamayori-hime.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Toyotama-hime plays a crucial role in establishing the divine lineage of the Japanese Imperial family. As the mother of Ugayafukiaezu, she is the grandmother of Emperor Jimmu, the legendary founder of Japan. Her story highlights the connection between the human world and the divine realm, particularly the sea. She represents the power and mystery of the ocean, as well as the importance of honoring promises and respecting the boundaries between the mortal and immortal worlds. Her story also explores themes of love, loss, and the complexities of marriage between different realms.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Toyotama-hime's dragon form symbolizes her connection to the primordial forces of nature and the untamed power of the sea. Her shapeshifting ability reflects the transformative nature of life and the hidden aspects of reality. The act of Hoori peeking during her childbirth can be interpreted as a violation of sacred boundaries and a loss of innocence. The shame she experiences and her subsequent return to the sea represent the consequences of breaking trust and the inherent differences between the human and divine realms. She is a symbol of fertility, royalty and the power of the sea.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Toyotama-hime continues to be recognized in Japanese culture, often depicted in art and literature. Shrines dedicated to her can be found throughout Japan, particularly near the coast. She is often invoked for safe childbirth and marital harmony. Her story serves as a reminder of the importance of respecting nature and honoring ancient traditions. She is a representation of strong female figures in Japanese mythology, and her story continues to fascinate and inspire people today. She exemplifies the blending of human and divine ancestry within the Japanese Imperial lineage.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The Kojiki is the oldest extant chronicle in Japan, completed in 712 CE. It contains myths, legends, and historical accounts, including the story of Toyotama-hime.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The Nihon Shoki, also known as the Nihongi, is the second-oldest book of classical Japanese history. Completed in 720 CE, it provides an alternative account of Toyotama-hime's story.

associations.name: Sea
associations.significance: Toyotama-hime is a dragon princess of the sea, representing its power and mystery.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Her true form is a dragon, symbolizing her divine nature and connection to primordial forces.

associations.name: Fertility
associations.significance: She is associated with fertility and safe childbirth, being the mother of Ugayafukiaezu.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Sedna
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A sea goddess whose story involves a broken trust and a return to the sea.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Melusine
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A water spirit whose true form is revealed when her husband breaks a promise.

companions.name: Hoori
companions.description: A hunter and grandson of Amaterasu who married Toyotama-hime.

companions.name: Ryūjin
companions.description: The dragon king of the sea and father of Toyotama-hime.

companions.name: Tamayori-hime
companions.description: Younger sister of Toyotama-hime, who raises Ugayafukiaezu.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of rice, sake, and seafood are made at shrines dedicated to Toyotama-hime.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers for Safe Childbirth
worshipPractices.description: Pregnant women often pray to Toyotama-hime for a safe and healthy delivery.

festivals.name: Sea Festivals
festivals.description: Some coastal communities honor Toyotama-hime during local sea festivals, celebrating the bounty of the ocean.

cultural.modernLegacy: Toyotama-hime's story continues to be retold in modern media, including anime, manga, and video games, showcasing her enduring appeal as a powerful and mysterious figure in Japanese mythology.
